metadata.title: Rehearse to 9:30 + Nail the Live Demo
metadata.playlist: Make It Count — The Complete Brand System
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Capstone — Complete Brand System (200 pts, due 2026-08-14)

beats.narration_text: Ciao — this is Liam. Episode 5 of 6. The last mile. Everything is built. Everything is live. Now the question is whether you can deliver it under pressure in exactly the right amount of time. Practice until the timing is automatic and the demo is boring because it always works. This episode covers three things: timing discipline, professional presence, and the bonus. Let's go.

props.segment: Rehearse to 9:30 + Nail the Live Demo
props.command: Identify my most likely overrun sections and demo failure points from my rehearsal description.
props.runningText: practice until the timing is automatic and the demo is boring because it always works…
output: 9:30 target — where points are lost and how to cut
The bonus — what it rewards and what it doesn't

beats.narration_text: Time yourself. Not 'roughly ten minutes' — measured. The target is nine minutes thirty seconds. Going over ten minutes loses points. Not a warning, not a note — points off. The way you hit nine thirty is not by rushing at the end — it is by cutting during rehearsal. Record one full run before presentation day. Play it back. Find the section that ran long. Cut it. Not the content — the words. The same idea in fewer words. Record again. Repeat until the recording says nine twenty to nine thirty-five. If you get to presentation day and you have never recorded a full run, you do not know how long your presentation is — and you will find out at the worst possible moment. The sections most likely to run long: storytelling and the tool demo. Storytelling runs long because the story feels important and the presenter does not want to cut it. The demo runs long because features break and recovery takes time. Build that time into the clock. Cut the story to fit. Record until it fits.

props.artifactHeading: Over 10 minutes = points off. Record until it fits.
artifactLines: Target: 9:30. Hard cap: 10:00. Points off for over — not a warning.
Method: record one full run · play back · find the long section · cut words not content · record again.
Pass condition: recording reads 9:20–9:35 consistently.
Most likely overruns: storytelling (feels important) + demo (recovery time).
If you haven't recorded a full run, you don't know how long your presentation is.
props.sparkLine: Record. Cut. Record again. Stop when it reads 9:20–9:35.

beats.act: professional presence — organized, confident, one memorable moment
beats.narration_text: Professional presence. Organized means the grader never waits for you to find something — transitions between sections are planned, the demo is open before you start, and slide navigation is practiced. Confident means you do not read slides aloud. The slides are not your script — they are visual support for what you are saying. If you need to read a slide to know what to say, you are not ready to present yet. Engaging means your voice is not flat and your pacing is not uniform — you slow down for the thing that matters, you speed up through the things that are context. And one memorable moment. Not the whole presentation — one moment that is different from the standard recitation. The moment you ask the audience a question and then answer it. The moment you say 'here is the thing most people get wrong about this.' The moment the demo does the thing that makes somebody in the room go — oh, that is clever. Plan the memorable moment. It does not happen by accident.

artifactLines: Organized: transitions planned · demo open before you start · navigation practiced.
Confident: slides are visual support, not your script. If you need to read them, not ready yet.
Engaging: slow for the thing that matters · fast through context · voice is not flat.
One memorable moment: plan it — it doesn't happen by accident.
The memorable moment: a question you answer · a common mistake named · a demo that surprises.
props.sparkLine: One memorable moment per presentation. Plan it — accidental is not a plan.

beats.act: the bonus — bold creative risk, not doing requirements well
beats.narration_text: The bonus. Five to ten extra points for a bold creative risk nobody else took. This is the most misunderstood part of the capstone brief. The bonus is not for doing the requirements well — it is not for having a coherent brand or a working demo. Those are expected. The bonus is for one thing that is genuinely unexpected: a format, a reveal, a piece of creative execution that makes the grader think 'I haven't seen that before.' What does not qualify: a beautiful slide deck, a polished brand, a demo that works smoothly. What qualifies: presenting a brand story from the perspective of your primary persona instead of yourself. Opening with a live interaction with the tool instead of a framing slide. Turning the UVP into a one-sentence challenge to the room. The bonus requires creative risk — something that could fail publicly. Aim for one. One is enough. And the one memorable moment from the last beat and the bonus are often the same thing.

props.artifactTitle: The bonus — +5 to +10 for bold creative risk
props.artifactHeading: Not for doing requirements well. For something nobody else did.
artifactLines: Bonus = bold creative risk that could fail publicly — not polish, not competence.
Does NOT qualify: beautiful deck · coherent brand · demo that works smoothly.
Qualifies: story from persona's POV · opening with live tool interaction · UVP as a challenge to the room.
Aim for one bold move — one is enough.
The memorable moment and the bonus are often the same thing.
props.sparkLine: The bonus rewards a risk that could fail — not work done well.

beats.narration_text: Verdict for Rehearse to Nine Thirty and Nail the Live Demo. Nine thirty is the target. Ten is the hard cap — points off for going over. Record a full run, find the long sections, cut words not content, record again until it fits. Professional presence: organized transitions, no slide reading, one planned memorable moment. The bonus rewards a bold creative risk — not doing the requirements well — and you need exactly one. Episode 6 is the final submission checklist.

props.artifactTitle: Rehearse to 9:30 + Nail the Live Demo
artifactLines: 9:30 target · 10:00 hard cap · points off for over.
Method: record full run → find long section → cut words → record until 9:20–9:35.
Presence: no slide reading · organized transitions · one planned memorable moment.
Bonus: bold risk that could fail publicly — not polish or competence.
One bold move is enough. The memorable moment and the bonus are often the same.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Before you rehearse again, let Claude identify where you are most likely to lose time and where the demo is most likely to fail. Paste this prompt with your rehearsal notes filled in. The prompt reads: I am preparing for the INFO 7375 capstone presentation. I have a completed deck and a planned demo. I need you to identify my most likely overrun sections and demo failure points so I can rehearse to fix them before presentation day. Here is my deck structure and estimated timing per section. Brand Intro: [estimated time]. Strategy section: [estimated time]. Visual Identity section: [estimated time]. Brand Storytelling section: [estimated time and a two-sentence description of the story I am telling]. Tool Demo section: [estimated time and a description of the two or three features I am demonstrating in order]. Here is my demo plan. Tool URL: [paste]. Features in order: [list]. Any step that involves a paid API call: [yes or no — if yes, describe]. Recorded fallback: [yes or no]. Here is whether I have recorded a full run yet: [yes or no — if yes, describe the result; if no, say so]. Based on this, tell me: which section is most likely to overrun based on the content I described, what is the single most likely failure point in the demo based on the features I listed, and what is one bold creative move I have not mentioned that would fit the format and be genuinely unexpected for a capstone presentation. After you get the response, fix the overrun first — cut words from that section before you rehearse again.

props.segment: Rehearse to 9:30 + Nail the Live Demo
props.command: Deck section timings · story description · demo features in order · paid API call? · fallback? · recorded full run yet? Identify: most likely overrun section · most likely demo failure point · one bold creative move that would be genuinely unexpected.
props.runningText: fix the overrun first — cut words from that section before you rehearse again…

beats.narration_text: Rehearse to Nine Thirty and Nail the Live Demo. Liam, in for Bear.
